verses.commentary: The promise fulfilled. The LORD was gracious means visited, attended to. As he had said. God keeps his word. What he had promised. This has been building since chapter 12.
Sarah conceived. Bore a son. In his old age. At the very time, the appointed time. God's timing is exact. The impossible has happened.
Named Isaac, he laughs. The name recalls Abraham's laughter (17:17) and Sarah's (18:12). Joy replaces doubt. Laughter of fulfillment.
Obedience to the covenant sign. Eight days as commanded (17:12). Abraham obeys exactly.
One hundred years old. Twenty-five years since the initial promise (12:4). The long wait ends. Faith vindicated.
Sarah's response. Laughter now means joy, not mockery or doubt. Everyone who hears will laugh with me. Joy shared. The miracle is public.
Who would have said? The rhetorical question emphasizes the impossibility. Sarah nurses. I have borne him a son. Personal triumph. In his old age. The miracle is complete.
Isaac grows. Weaning (typically 2-3 years old) is celebrated. A great feast marks the milestone. Isaac is thriving.
Trouble emerges. Sarah sees Ishmael (now about 16-17) mocking, the same root as Isaac's name. Mocking or playing? The text is ambiguous, but Sarah perceives a threat.
Sarah's demand. Get rid of, expel, drive out. That slave woman and her son, harsh language. Will never share in the inheritance. Sarah protects Isaac's rights. This is both protective and cruel.
Abraham's distress. Greatly. Because it concerned his son. Abraham loves Ishmael. The demand tears him apart.

God intervenes. Do not be distressed. Listen to Sarah. God affirms Sarah's demand, not because of her harshness but for covenant purposes. Through Isaac your offspring will be reckoned. Isaac is the line of promise. Paul quotes this in Romans 9:7 and Hebrews 11:18.
Promise for Ishmael. A nation also. God will bless Ishmael. Because he is your offspring. God honors Abraham's fatherhood. Ishmael is not abandoned.

Early next morning, immediate obedience despite pain. Food and water, minimal provision. Set them on her shoulders. Sent her off. Abraham obeys God but the scene is heartbreaking. She wandered, lost in the desert.
Water runs out. She puts the boy under a bush for shade. Death looms.
She distances herself. A bowshot away, close but not watching. I cannot watch the boy die. Maternal anguish. She began to sob. Despair.

God heard the boy. Not just Hagar but Ishmael. The angel calls from heaven. What is the matter? Do not be afraid. God has heard, echoing Ishmael's name (God hears). As he lies there, dying but not dead.
Command and promise. Lift the boy up. Take him by the hand, tender care. I will make him into a great nation. The promise of 17:20 is reaffirmed. Ishmael has a future.
God opened her eyes. The well was there all along. Divine provision revealed. She filled the skin. Gave the boy a drink. Life is restored.
God was with the boy. Divine presence. He grew up. Lived in the desert, echoing the wild donkey prophecy (16:12). Became an archer, a wilderness skill.
Desert of Paran, in the Sinai. His mother got a wife from Egypt, her homeland. Ishmael's line continues. The Ishmaelites become the Arab peoples.
Transition to new scene. Abimelek (same from chapter 20 or his successor). God is with you. The pagan king recognizes divine blessing.
Abimelek requests an oath. Swear before God. Do not deal falsely, lie or deceive. Show the same kindness I showed you. Abimelek recalls his generosity (20:14-15).
Abraham agrees. Simple oath.
Before the oath, a grievance. A well seized. Water rights are critical in arid regions.
Abimelek's innocence. I don't know. You didn't tell me. First I'm hearing of it. Honest response.
Abraham gives animals. The two men made a treaty (berit), a covenant. Formal agreement between equals or near-equals.
Seven ewe lambs set apart. Specific, ceremonial.
Question about the seven lambs. What is the meaning?
The seven lambs as witness. Proof that Abraham dug the well. Physical testimony to ownership.
Named Beersheba, well of the oath or well of seven. Wordplay on seven (sheva) and oath (shevuah). The place commemorates the treaty.
The treaty concluded. Abimelek returns. Peaceful separation.

Abraham plants a tree. Tamarisk is long-living, symbolic of permanence. He called on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God (El Olam). Worship marks the place. Eternal God emphasizes God's everlasting nature.
Abraham stays. For a long time. He's settling, though still as a foreigner. The promise is not yet fully realized but Abraham remains in the land.
